ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Chinese sausage stir-fried cabbage; mapped to the broader stir-fried food product class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Stir-fried mixed dish with meat and vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for Chinese sausage stir-fried cabbage; A0CKP (stir-fried mixed dish with meat and vegetables) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 category anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of Chinese sausage stir-fried cabbage is approximately 300 g, typical of a shared side dish or individual portion at a Chinese zi char stall or home meal in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Nutrient Composition of Food; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Pork sausage and cabbage components
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board, Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB or USDA entry exists for Chinese sausage stir-fried cabbage as a composite dish. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo entries for stir-fried cabbage dishes and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for Chinese-style pork sausage (lap cheong) and green cabbage, combined using a representative recipe ratio of approximately 60% cabbage, 25% Chinese sausage, and 15% sauce and oil. The input record values (energy 126.67 kcal, protein 5.0 g, fat 9.33 g, carbohydrate 6.0 g per 100 g) are consistent with this component-based estimation and have been retained with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database — cabbage and low-carbohydrate stir-fried vegetable dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chinese sausage stir-fried cabbage. GI estimated at 32 based on the dominant low-GI cruciferous vegetable base (cabbage GI ~10) combined with the modest carbohydrate contribution from Chinese sausage (added sugars) and oyster sauce. The composite GI is anchored to the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked cabbage and low-carbohydrate stir-fried vegetable dishes (GI range 15–45). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (32 × 18.0) / 100 = 5.76, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 6.0 × 3 = 18.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g from Chinese sausage, oyster sauce, and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish as served; request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce, substitute low-sodium soy sauce, and reduce the quantity of Chinese sausage to bring sodium below 200 mg per 100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugars in Chinese sausage and oyster sauce despite low overall carbohydrate load of 6 g per 100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate content (6 g per 100 g) and low GI (estimated 32) make this dish broadly compatible with type 2 diabetes management, but individuals should request reduced Chinese sausage quantity and sugar-free oyster sauce to minimise added sugar intake, and pair with a high-fibre side to further blunt postprandial glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 3.33 g per 100 g and sodium 400 mg per 100 g from processed Chinese sausage; trans fat 0.07 g per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chinese sausage (lap cheong) is a processed pork product contributing saturated fat, trans fat, and high sodium, all of which are established cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should reduce or omit the sausage, use minimal oil, and request low-sodium condiments to lower the overall atherogenic and hypertensive load of this dish.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid and saturated fat from processed pork sausage amplify TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise Chinese sausage in this dish due to its high arachidonic acid and saturated fat content, which directly promote systemic inflammation; the cabbage component provides anti-inflammatory sulforaphane and may be consumed as a standalone stir-fry without the sausage.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chinese Sausage Stir-Fried Cabbage (腊肠炒包菜; Malay: Kobis Goreng dengan Sosej Cina) is a classic Chinese home-style and zi char stir-fry dish popular in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora in Southeast Asia. The dish is prepared by wok-frying sliced green cabbage (bao cai) at high heat with sliced lap cheong (Chinese cured pork sausage), minced garlic, oyster sauce, light soy sauce, and a small amount of Shaoxing rice wine, producing a dish with tender-crisp cabbage, savoury-sweet caramelised sausage, and a lightly glossy sauce. The dominant flavour profile is umami-rich and mildly sweet from the lap cheong, with a subtle wok hei (breath of the wok) smokiness. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 127 kcal, 5 g protein, 9.3 g fat (3.3 g saturated), 6 g carbohydrate, 1.7 g dietary fibre, and 400 mg sodium. The high sodium content is the primary clinical concern, driven by the combination of Chinese sausage, oyster sauce, and soy sauce, making this dish unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease without significant modification. The low carbohydrate load and low estimated glycemic index (approximately 32) make it broadly compatible with type 2 diabetes management when portion size is controlled and added sugars in condiments are minimised. Cabbage contributes vitamin C (13.3 mg per 100 g) and sulforaphane, offering modest anti-inflammatory and antioxidant benefit. A standard plate serving is approximately 300 g, providing 380 kcal and 1200 mg sodium.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for Chinese sausage stir-fried cabbage as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo stir-fried vegetable dish entries and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy pork sausage and cabbage components using a representative recipe ratio. The input record values are consistent with this component-based estimation and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate anchored to University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed journal. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adapted source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite Chinese stir-fried dish are not available as a single entry in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.07 g per 100 g, a trace level consistent with the processed pork sausage component; this is a measured value from the input record and is not zero. All core macros (protein_g 5.0, fat_g 9.33, carbohydrate_g 6.0) are non-zero and consistent with the dish composition. The input record listed the dish name as 腊肉炒包菜 (using 腊肉 meaning cured pork/bacon); the canonical Chinese name has been corrected to 腊肠炒包菜 (using 腊肠 meaning Chinese sausage/lap cheong) to accurately reflect the primary protein ingredient. The original input alias 腊肉炒包菜 is retained in the aliases array for retrieval continuity. This dish is not halal as it contains pork-based Chinese sausage.
